content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 17, 2018

Bill Sartor
City Attorney
City of Tracy
333 Civic Center Plaza
Tracy, CA 95376

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-252

Dear Mr. Sartor:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Tracy Planning Commissioner Albert "Clift" Hudson, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Moreover, we base this letter on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Commissioner Hudson from taking part in planning commission decisions regarding a large residential development project approximately 600 feet from his residence?

CONCLUSION

Based upon the information provided and the fact that Commissioner Hudson's property is just 600 feet away from the project and located on a street that currently ends at an undeveloped lot adjacent to the project, it appears that the proposed 600-unit housing project may foreseeably result in a substantial change in traffic levels and the intensity of use, which would also likely influence the market value of Commissioner Hudson's property. Accordingly, the Act prohibits Commissioner Hudson from making, participating in making, or using his position to influencing any decisions regarding the project.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-252
Page No. 2

FACTS

The City of Tracy is approximately 22.28 square miles and has an estimated population of
89,000. The Tracy Planning Commission has five members and its duties include making findings
and providing recommendations to the City Council regarding development applications.
Commissioner Hudson was appointed to the Commission on March 15, 2016.

Commissioner Hudson's primary residence is located approximately 600 feet from the
Tracy Village Project. The Project is an active adult, gated, master-planned residential community
encompassing 135.2 acres. The Project will include up to 600 single-family detached homes and an
interconnected lake and open space system. It will be the first-of-its kind development in the City.
The City's Development Services Department has been processing a development application for
the Project following a successful initiative in December 2015 that amended the Tracy Municipal
Code and General Plan to allocate 600 residential allotments to the Project.

The Project is currently undergoing an environmental review process that consists of
preparing and circulating a Draft Environmental Impact Report. Upon completion of the
environmental review, a General Plan amendment, a Specific Plan, a Tentative Map, a zoning
ordinance amendment, and an annexation request will be presented to the Commission for its
review and recommendation. Additional approvals for the Project such as a development review
permit for architecture will also be presented to the Commission at a later date.

Commissioner Hudson's primary residence is located approximately 600 feet from the
Project, in a single family residential neighborhood built in the late 1980s and early 1990s. Based
upon the map provided, the Commissioner's residence is located on a street that currently ends at an
undeveloped lot adjacent to the property on which the Project is proposed, which also appears
mostly undeveloped.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any state or local public official from making, participating in
making, or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in which the official
has a financial interest specified in Section 87103. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
government decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section
87103.) Of the interests recognized under the Act, the only interest you have indicated is an interest
in real property resulting from Commissioner Hudson's residence. (Section 87103(b).)

Foreseeability:

In this case, Commissioner Hudson's residence is not explicitly involved in decisions
regarding the Project. Accordingly, the foreseeability standard is provided in Regulation 18701(b),
which states:

"A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic

File No. A-17-252
Page No. 3

possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If
the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not
subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable."

Materiality:

Regulation 18705.2(a) provides a list of circumstances under which the reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on real property in which an official has a
financial interest is material. As relevant to your facts, the financial effect will be material if the
decisions:

"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air
quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of
the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest.

[¶]…[¶]

"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the
market value of the official's property."

Based upon the information provided, Commissioner Hudson's primary residence is located
approximately 600 feet from the Project on a street that currently ends at an undeveloped lot
adjacent to the property on which the Project is proposed. The site of the Project also appears to be
mostly undeveloped. Without further facts, it appears that the proposed 600-unit housing project
may foreseeably result in a substantial change in traffic levels and the intensity of use, which would
also likely influence the market value of Commissioner Hudson's property.²

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

BGL:jgl

² For example, in the Stewart Advice Letter, No. A-15-030, we previously determined that an official with an
interest in a residence, a half mile from a 600-unit housing project, was disqualified from decisions regarding the
project due to the potential traffic concerns if the street on which the official's residence was located was used to access
the project in an adjacent neighborhood.

in planning commission decisions regarding a large residential development project approximately 
600 feet from his residence?

Commissioner Hudson from making, participating in making, or using his position to influencing 
any decisions regarding the project. 
The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are conlained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are lo Tille 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
